title: Opening Aces from Any Position
actionHistory: You are UTG. No action yet.

options.optionExplanation: Always raise with pocket aces. You want to build the pot preflop with the best possible starting hand.
Limping with aces is a classic beginner mistake. You miss value and let opponents see cheap flops with speculative hands.
5x is too large. A standard 2.5x raise builds the pot while keeping opponents in with worse hands.

explanation: Pocket aces is the best starting hand in Hold'em and should always be raised from every position. The common beginner mistake is limping to 'trap' — but this is terrible because it lets 4-5 players see a cheap flop, dramatically reducing your equity. Against one opponent, AA wins ~85% of the time. Against five, it drops to ~50%. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind builds the pot, narrows the field to 1-2 callers, and lets you play a manageable pot with a huge equity edge. Premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ) are the core of every position's opening range.
keyConceptSummary: Always raise premium pairs. Never limp — you want fewer opponents and a bigger pot when you have the best hand.
villainRange: Any single opponent: ~15% equity against AA

title: Suited Connectors from Late Position
actionHistory: Folds to you on the BTN.

options.optionExplanation: Opening 76s from the button is standard. Suited connectors have excellent playability and you have position.
Folding suited connectors on the button is too tight. These hands are profitable opens from late position.
Limping wastes your positional advantage. Raise to pressure the blinds.

explanation: 7-6 suited is a hand that beginners often fold everywhere or play from any position — both are wrong. From the button, it's a clear open. Suited connectors have value because they can flop straights, flushes, two pairs, and strong draws. Even when they miss, they have backdoor equity that lets you barrel profitably. The key is that this value only materializes with position — from UTG, 76s is a fold because you'll be out of position and the speculative nature of the hand requires seeing cheap flops and maneuvering postflop, which position enables. Range-based thinking means understanding WHY suited connectors work from certain spots.
keyConceptSummary: Suited connectors are profitable from late position because they combine playability with positional advantage. Fold them from EP.

title: Folding KJo from Early Position
actionHistory: You are UTG. No action yet.

options.optionExplanation: Folding is correct. KJo from UTG has too many domination problems — AK, AJ, KQ all crush you.
Opening KJo UTG looks reasonable but leads to tough spots. When you flop a king, AK has you dominated. When you flop a jack, AJ and QJ dominate.
Limping KJo from UTG is the worst option. You're playing a dominated hand passively out of position.

explanation: King-Jack offsuit looks like a good hand — two face cards! But from UTG, it's a clear fold. The problem is domination: when someone behind you has AK, AJ, KQ, or even KTs, your KJo is in terrible shape. If you flop a king, AK has you outkicked. If you flop a jack, AJ dominates you. And from UTG, the remaining 5-6 players have plenty of opportunities to hold these dominating hands. This is the trap of offsuit broadways from early position — they look pretty but play poorly because of kicker issues compounded by positional disadvantage. KJs (suited) is marginally better because of flush potential, but KJo should go in the muck.
keyConceptSummary: Offsuit broadway hands like KJo are traps from early position due to domination. Save them for late position.
villainRange: Players behind: many hands that dominate KJo (AK, AJ, KQ)

title: 3-Betting AQo vs a Middle Position Open
actionHistory: MP raises to 25. Folds to you on BTN.

options.optionExplanation: 3-betting AQo from the button is standard. You have a strong hand, position, and can build the pot or win it preflop.
Calling is okay but leaves the blinds in and doesn't define villain's range. 3-betting is more profitable.
Folding AQo on the button vs an MP open is absurdly tight.

explanation: AQ offsuit on the button facing a middle position raise is a standard 3-bet. Against MP's opening range, AQ has strong equity — roughly 55%. By 3-betting, you accomplish three things: you build the pot with a strong hand, you force out the blinds who might have otherwise called, and you narrow MP's range so you have better information postflop. If MP folds, you win the pot. If they call, you're in position with a premium hand. If they 4-bet, you can call or fold depending on your read. Range thinking tells you that AQo is firmly in the top portion of hands you'd play here, making it a value 3-bet.
keyConceptSummary: AQo in position is a standard 3-bet hand. It has the equity and playability to build big pots when you have the positional advantage.
villainRange: MP opening range: TT+, ATs+, AJo+, KQs, KJs, QJs

title: Big Blind Defense with a Marginal Hand
actionHistory: CO raises to 25. SB folds. Action on you in BB.

options.optionExplanation: Folding is correct. 96o is too weak to defend even getting a good price. It has poor playability and no real equity.
Calling with 96o is a losing play. You'll flop garbage most of the time and have no backdoor draws.
3-betting 96o as a bluff from the BB is too wide. Save your bluff 3-bets for suited hands with blockers.

explanation: While you should defend your big blind fairly wide, you shouldn't defend with everything. 96 offsuit is below the threshold because it has poor playability — it rarely flops anything strong, it can't make flushes, and its straight possibilities are limited. Compare this to 96 suited, which at least has flush potential and would be a borderline defend. The lesson in range construction is that not every hand deserves defense just because you're getting a price. Hands need playability — the ability to flop strong hands or draws — to justify calling out of position. Offsuit, disconnected, low hands should be folded.
keyConceptSummary: Not every hand deserves a BB defense. Offsuit junk with poor playability should be folded even at good pot odds.

title: Set Mining with Small Pocket Pairs
actionHistory: MP raises to 25. Folds to you on BTN.

options.optionExplanation: Calling to set mine is correct. You'll flop a set ~12% of the time, and when you do, you'll often win a big pot.
Folding pocket fours with position and deep stacks is too tight. The implied odds of flopping a set are excellent.
3-betting pocket fours is too aggressive. If you get 4-bet, you must fold. Just call and see a flop.

explanation: Small pocket pairs (22-66) are played primarily for their set-mining value. You'll flop a set about 12% of the time (roughly 1 in 8 attempts). When you do, you have a disguised monster that can win a huge pot. The math works when you have deep stacks (at least 15x the call amount) and position. Here you're calling 25 with 975 behind — that's a 39-to-1 ratio, which is excellent for set mining. When you miss the set (88% of the time), you simply check-fold. The key concept: some hands in your range exist purely for their implied odds — the money you'll win after the flop, not the direct pot odds preflop.
keyConceptSummary: Small pocket pairs are profitable calls with deep stacks and position, purely for set-mining implied odds.
villainRange: MP opening range: broad, includes many overpairs to your fours

title: Suited vs. Offsuit — Why It Matters
actionHistory: Folds to you in the CO.

options.optionExplanation: A5s from the CO is a standard open. The suited aspect gives you nut flush potential, making this playable.
Folding A5s from the cutoff is too tight. Suited aces have real value from late position.
Limping gives up initiative and lets the blinds see a free flop.

explanation: Ace-five suited is a profitable open from the cutoff, while ace-five offsuit would be marginal at best. The difference? Suitedness. Being suited adds roughly 3-4% raw equity, but more importantly, it adds playability — you can flop nut flush draws that give you excellent semi-bluffing opportunities, backdoor flush draws that provide extra equity, and when you hit a flush, it's always the nuts (ace-high flush). This playability transforms a mediocre hand into a profitable one. In range construction, suited aces are included in late position ranges while their offsuit counterparts are often excluded. That seemingly small suited/offsuit distinction has real monetary impact.
keyConceptSummary: Suited hands are significantly more valuable than offsuit. A5s is a standard open from late position; A5o is marginal at best.

title: Squeezing from the Big Blind
actionHistory: CO raises to 25. BTN calls 25. SB folds. Action on you in BB.

options.optionExplanation: Squeezing with AKs is textbook. You have a premium hand, and the flat caller is capped — they'd have 3-bet with their best hands.
Calling creates a multiway pot out of position. AKs plays much better as a 3-bet to isolate.
Folding AKs is never correct in any reasonable scenario.

explanation: A 'squeeze' is a 3-bet made after someone opens and someone else calls. It's called a squeeze because you're putting pressure on both players. The flat caller is in a tough spot because they showed a capped range by just calling (with premium hands, they would have 3-bet themselves). AK suited is a perfect squeezing hand — it's strong enough to want to get money in, and by squeezing, you often win the dead money from both the opener and the caller. Even when called, AKs has excellent equity and can flop the nut flush draw. The concept: when a player flat calls a raise, their range is defined and limited, making them vulnerable to a squeeze.
keyConceptSummary: Squeeze with premium hands when a player flat calls behind the opener. The flat caller's capped range makes them vulnerable.
villainRange: CO open: broad range. BTN flat: capped, no premiums

title: UTG Opening Range — Keep It Tight
actionHistory: You are UTG. No action yet.

options.optionExplanation: Folding 87s from UTG is correct. Even though it's suited, you have 5-6 players behind you and will be out of position the entire hand.
Opening 87s from UTG is too loose. You'll face 3-bets frequently and play every postflop street at a disadvantage.
Limping encourages multiway pots where you're first to act with a speculative hand. Terrible.

explanation: 87 suited is a great hand from the button, but it's a clear fold from UTG. Your UTG opening range should be roughly: AA-77, AKs-ATs, AKo-AJo, KQs, KJs, QJs. That's about 12-15% of all hands. Why so tight? Because five or six players act after you, increasing the chance of running into a premium hand or being 3-bet. Even when you get to see a flop, you'll be out of position, negating the playability that makes suited connectors valuable. Range construction means understanding that each position has a different optimal range, and UTG is by far the tightest.
keyConceptSummary: UTG opening range is roughly 12-15% of hands. Suited connectors need position to be profitable — fold them from early position.

title: Why Limping Is Almost Always Wrong
actionHistory: UTG folds. You are in MP.

options.optionExplanation: Raising is correct with JTs from MP. You have a playable hand and should raise or fold, never limp.
Limping JTs signals weakness, allows everyone to see a cheap flop, and gives up the initiative.
Folding JTs from MP is slightly too tight. This hand has enough playability to open from middle position.

explanation: JT suited is a playable hand from MP, and the correct play is to raise — not limp. Open limping (calling the big blind instead of raising) is one of the biggest leaks in beginner poker for several reasons. First, it shows weakness, inviting raises from players behind you. Second, it creates cheap multiway pots where your speculative hand doesn't have the equity edge that a raised pot provides. Third, you lose the initiative — if you had raised and everyone called, you can c-bet the flop and represent strength. By limping, you give up this option. The fundamental rule: if a hand is worth playing, it's worth raising. If it's not worth raising, fold it.
keyConceptSummary: Raise or fold — never limp. If a hand is worth playing, it's worth raising to take the initiative and thin the field.
villainRange: Players behind: various, likely to raise over a limp
